notes: Directly explains the anchor upsell: premium offer first, 5-10x higher, same primary features, then cheaper core offer.
Useful background on upsells and add-ons after a first sale, but it does not distinguish anchor versus menu.
Defines both menu upsell and anchor upsell, directly supporting the choice between a prescribed menu and a premium anchor.
Gives direct rationale and examples for keeping premium anchor offers ready even when most customers choose the core option.
Mentions the anchor upsell pattern, but most of the chunk summarizes unrelated downsell and continuity offers.
Directly explains offering premium items first, using 5-10x anchoring, and changing only secondary features.
Directly compares menu and anchor contexts and lays out the anchor steps, making it highly cite-worthy for the decision.
Directly addresses stretching terms when the buyer still wants the offer, including front-loaded payment splits and a pivot point.
Focuses on trial-with-penalty mechanics and pay-less-now comparison, not the payment-plan versus feature-downsell choice in the query.
Directly summarizes feature downsells and says to use payment plan downselling when the customer wants the chosen feature suite but cannot afford it today.
Directly explains that the issue is often what they pay now, not total price, and recommends changing terms rather than selling cheaper.
Provides useful background on spreading payments and seesaw downselling, but it is less focused on the specific lower-price/remove-features decision.
Directly defines feature downsells as lowering price by changing what the customer gets through quantity, quality, alternatives, or removing components.
Directly contrasts payment plan downsells with feature downsells in summary form, which answers the confusable choice.
Directly supports stretching terms: payment plans get buyers to pay full price over time and avoid framing it as a penalty.
Directly explains the feature-downsell process and when to cross back into payment plan downselling if they still want the offer.
Contains background examples of downsell flows and prepayment, but it is broad and not strong evidence for this specific objection.
Directly addresses free offers lowering lead cost while creating too much volume if the business is not prepared to follow up.
Useful for factoring service/support cost into profitability, but it does not diagnose free-offer lead quality directly.
Background on CAC levers and free/new as advertising tools; it sets context but does not address the support overload problem.
Useful background for calculating fully loaded CAC from leads and sales, but not direct evidence about whether the free workshop is working.
Discusses discount offers and card-on-file mechanics, not the free workshop volume, booking-rate, or support-load diagnosis.
Directly addresses the free-offer myth with test results and the create flow, monetize, then add friction principle.
Some background on monetizing within the first 30 days, but it is not focused on free lead overload or qualification friction.
Directly says free volume can be wasteful without manpower and recommends adding qualification friction after creating flow.
Broad background on lead costs and making more money per customer; only indirectly useful for diagnosing the workshop economics.
Defines and calculates CAC, but does not provide the client-financed acquisition levels.
Only mentions that levels were covered and then shifts to teaching method; it does not answer the exact levels.
Closing remarks and calls to action; no evidence about CFA levels.
Mentions level three and transition into money models, but it is too fragmentary to answer the levels by itself.
Discusses attraction offers and demand for CFA, but not the three client-financed acquisition levels.
Directly explains level two and level three thresholds using 30-day gross profit versus CAC, including the 2x CAC target.
Defines CFA and the first-30-day gross profit idea, but does not enumerate the levels.
Directly lists the advertising/CFA levels, especially level one and level two, and sets up level three.
Provides concrete use cases for pay-less-now/pay-more-later across workshops, physical products, and local services.
Directly summarizes the offer: delayed full-price payment with guarantee versus discounted pay-now option, plus criteria and cancellation guidance.
Directly explains how to present the pay-now option after pay-later acceptance and summarizes the mechanics.
Directly explains why the model works: advertise free, get card on file, then offer discounted pay-now or higher pay-later.
Directly states when to use pay-less-now/pay-more-later versus trial with penalty: physical products or one-time services versus recurring/customer-work products.
Shows pay-less-now/pay-more-later in a larger money model example, but it is background rather than a direct rule for when to use it.
Directly gives optimization and recurring-revenue guidance for when and how to use the pay-now versus pay-later structure.
Covers payment plan downsells, a different mechanism from pay-less-now/pay-more-later.
Directly defines pay-less-now/pay-more-later and explains the risk-removal, delayed-payment, guarantee, and pay-now discount logic.
Directly summarizes rollover upsells for old, upset, competitor, and current customers, including crediting prior purchases toward a new offer.
Directly gives scripts for crediting old or upset customers toward the better thing they actually wanted.
Directly addresses old customers and win-backs, including offering prior-purchase credit and urgency to return.
Mostly describes a gift-card referral play, though the summary includes rollover audiences such as old and upset customers.
Discusses upsell timing and deprivation, not how to handle upset previous customers with a new better offer.
Directly explains using rollover upsells to reengage old customers and rescue upset customers instead of refunding.
Directly explains how to credit past purchases toward a more expensive new offer and gives a win-back example.
Useful background on third-party financing and introduces layaway, but it does not fully explain pay-before-access in this chunk.
Directly states the customer does not get the thing until they pay, which aligns incentives and avoids the business taking financing risk.
Directly defines layaway as paying off the product before getting it and says the customer takes the risk, not the business.
Some background on prepayment framing and spreading payments, but it does not clearly tie payment to withholding access.
Useful background on front-loading payments, but it does not answer the pay-over-time-before-access layaway question directly.
Explains the risk when the business starts delivering before full payment, but the layaway solution is only background here.
Summary mentions layaway and scheduled payments, but it is not as direct as the chunks defining layaway and no-access-until-paid.
Directly answers the month-four churn case by recommending a lifetime discount earned just past the common churn point and reminders before it.
Directly explains using waived fees and commitments so leaving costs more than staying through the initial churn point.
Directly useful for retention: apply discounts or free time at the end so customers earn it by paying through the term.
Useful background on converting short-term buyers into lower-monthly long-term continuity, but not specific to month-four churn.
Directly recommends status or bonus milestones just after known churn points to pull members through the risky months.
Directly explains commitment terms, cancellation fees, and guardrails that keep customers through rough months.
Directly says waived-fee commitments can get customers through the four-to-six-month churn point and remove later financial reasons to leave.
Directly supports retention by making the cost to quit exceed the cost to stay, while warning not to mask product problems.
Useful background on recurring member bonuses and ongoing benefits, but it is less specific to the month-four churn timing.
Provides background on continuity discounts and cancellation policy, but its upfront-free-time example is not the best fit for high churn.
Directly supports front-loading payments within the first 30 days so fulfillment and acquisition cash is recovered sooner.
Directly recommends layaway/no delivery until paid, notes it aligns incentives, and says the business does not take on risk.
Directly defines layaway for products and states the customer takes the risk instead of the business.
Directly useful for handling lower-payment requests: lower monthly payments require more down payment, and custom seesaw plans need care.
Useful background on front-loading and seesaw framing, but it is less direct about immediate fulfillment costs than the layaway chunks.
Directly warns that if the business starts delivering before full payment, it takes on risk, and frames payment-plan pricing accordingly.
Only gives a high-level summary that payment plan downsells split payment over time; it lacks the physical-product cash-gap cautions.
Directly summarizes payment-plan profitability risks, layaway/financing options, schedule design, and cash-collected-today incentives.
Directly warns payment plans are a gamble and lose money when customers cancel before the business profits, relevant to immediate fulfillment costs.
